senses.definition: Timers that start or stop a device automatically at set times.
examples: The studio lights are controlled by autotimers to save energy.
Garden sprinklers with autotimers help you water your plants regularly.

senses.definition: Relating to autotomy, the shedding of a body part as a defensive response in animals.
examples: The lizard showed autotomic behavior when it dropped its tail to escape.
Autotomic responses can help certain species survive predator attacks.

senses.definition: Acts of autotomy, in which an animal sheds a body part to escape danger.
examples: Researchers recorded several autotomies during the predator–prey trials.
In some species, repeated autotomies can reduce the animal's long-term fitness.

senses.definition: To shed a body part deliberately as a defense mechanism.
examples: The gecko can autotomise its tail when threatened by a predator.
Crabs sometimes autotomise a claw to escape from a tight grip.

senses.definition: Shed a body part deliberately as a defense mechanism.
examples: The lizard autotomised its tail to distract the predator.
Several individuals autotomised limbs during the experiment.

senses.definition: Sheds a body part deliberately as a defense mechanism.
examples: The species often autotomises its tail when grasped.
If caught by the leg, the crab autotomises it and escapes.

senses.definition: Shedding a body part deliberately as a defense mechanism.
examples: By autotomising its tail, the lizard distracts the attacker.
Autotomising and later regenerating limbs can be energetically costly.

senses.definition: To shed a body part deliberately as a defense mechanism.
examples: When attacked, the lizard may autotomize its tail and run away.
Some spiders can autotomize legs to escape from webs or predators.

senses.definition: Shed a body part deliberately as a defense mechanism.
examples: The salamander autotomized its tail during the encounter.
Several specimens had autotomized limbs after repeated attacks.

senses.definition: Sheds a body part deliberately as a defense mechanism.
examples: The species autotomizes its tail when seized by predators.
If threatened, the octopus sometimes autotomizes an arm.

senses.definition: Shedding a body part deliberately as a defense mechanism.
examples: By autotomizing its tail, the lizard increases its chance of escape.
Autotomizing and regrowing tissues demands significant energy.

senses.definition: Able to shed a body part deliberately as a defense mechanism.
examples: Autotomous species can lose tails or limbs to avoid capture.
The autotomous tail continues to move after detachment.

senses.definition: The act of an animal shedding a body part on purpose, usually to escape a predator.
examples: Autotomy allows some lizards to drop their tails when attacked.
Crab claw autotomy is a common response to handling stress.

senses.definition: A medical condition in which someone is poisoned by toxic substances produced inside their own body.
examples: The doctors suspected autotoxaemia caused by intestinal bacteria.
Early descriptions of autotoxaemia linked it to many vague symptoms.

senses.definition: Cases or types of poisoning caused by toxic substances produced within the body.
examples: Early medical texts reported various autotoxaemias without clear evidence.
Researchers reviewed historical accounts of intestinal autotoxaemias.

senses.definition: Poisoning that happens when harmful substances made inside the body enter the blood.
examples: The patient was thought to be suffering from autotoxemia.
Classic theories of autotoxemia focused on toxins from the gut.

senses.definition: Instances or types of poisoning caused by toxins produced within the body.
examples: Several autotoxemias were described in early twentieth-century medicine.
The paper reviews different intestinal autotoxemias and their proposed treatments.

senses.definition: Causing poisoning to the body by toxins that the body itself produces.
examples: Autotoxic substances may accumulate in cases of liver failure.
The theory suggested that autotoxic products led to mental symptoms.

senses.definition: Poisoning of the body by toxic substances that are produced within the body itself, especially in the intestines.
examples: Autotoxication was once blamed for many vague health complaints.
Modern medicine views classic autotoxication theories with skepticism.

senses.definition: The harmful effect of toxins produced by an organism on that same organism.
examples: The researchers measured the autotoxicity of metabolic waste products.
In plants, autotoxicity can limit the growth of seedlings near the parent.

senses.definition: A condition in which an organism is made ill by toxic substances that it produces itself.
examples: The physician diagnosed autotoxicosis due to poor bowel function.
Some early psychiatrists linked mental illness to intestinal autotoxicosis.

senses.definition: A toxic substance that is produced inside an organism and can poison that same organism.
examples: The bacteria in the gut were thought to release an autotoxin.
Researchers attempted to identify the specific autotoxin responsible for the symptoms.

senses.definition: Toxic substances produced within an organism that can poison that same organism.
examples: Failure to excrete autotoxins may lead to serious disease.
The team studied how the liver clears autotoxins from the blood.

senses.definition: Poisoning of an organism by toxic substances produced within its own body.
examples: Early writers used the term autotoxis to describe self-poisoning from the intestines.
He believed that nervous disorders could arise from autotoxis.

senses.definition: A tractor that can move or operate largely on its own power, often without needing to be pulled by another vehicle.
examples: The old farm still had an early model autotractor in the shed.
Engineers demonstrated an autotractor designed for vineyard work.

senses.definition: An electrical transformer with one winding that acts as both the primary and the secondary coil.
examples: An autotransformer can be used to adjust the voltage supplied to equipment.
The engineer chose an autotransformer for its compact size and efficiency.

senses.definition: Electrical transformers that use a single winding as both primary and secondary.
examples: Autotransformers are often used where only a small voltage change is needed.
The plant upgraded its motor starters to modern autotransformers.

senses.definition: A medical procedure in which a person receives their own blood, collected and returned to their body.
examples: During surgery, autotransfusion reduced the need for donor blood.
The team used autotransfusion to recycle blood lost on the battlefield.

senses.definition: Medical procedures in which patients receive their own collected blood.
examples: Autotransfusions have become common in major orthopedic operations.
The study compared autotransfusions with traditional donor transfusions.

senses.definition: A transplant in which tissue or an organ is moved from one place to another in the same person.
examples: The surgeon performed an autotransplant of the patient's own kidney.
Bone autotransplant is preferred when suitable tissue is available.

senses.definition: To move tissue or an organ to a different place in the same person.
examples: The team decided to autotransplant the healthy kidney to a better position.
Dentists sometimes autotransplant teeth to fill gaps.

senses.definition: The process of moving tissue or an organ within the same person to treat disease or injury.
examples: Renal autotransplantation can be used to treat certain vascular problems.
Autotransplantation of bone marrow can restore blood cell production after chemotherapy.

senses.definition: The act of making a surgical opening in one’s own skull, usually regarded as extremely dangerous and rare.
examples: Historical reports of autotrepanation are controversial and disturbing.
Autotrepanation is strongly discouraged by medical professionals.

senses.definition: Having three sets of chromosomes that all come from the same species.
examples: The autotriploid plants showed reduced seed production.
An autotriploid cell carries three copies of each chromosome.

senses.definition: The state of having three sets of chromosomes from the same species.
examples: Autotriploidy can arise through errors in cell division.
Researchers studied the effects of autotriploidy on plant fertility.

senses.definition: An organism that makes its own food from simple substances like light, water, and carbon dioxide.
examples: Green plants are classic autotrophs that use sunlight to produce sugar.
Autotrophs form the base of most food chains in ecosystems.

senses.definition: Able to make organic food from simple inorganic substances.
examples: Autotrophic organisms convert light energy into chemical energy.
Some bacteria are autotrophic and live near deep-sea vents.

senses.definition: In a way that involves making organic food from simple inorganic substances.
examples: The algae can live autotrophically under suitable light conditions.
These bacteria grow autotrophically using chemical energy from minerals.

senses.definition: Types or examples of nutrition in which organisms make their own food from simple substances.
examples: Different autotrophies can be based on light or chemical energy.
The textbook describes several autotrophies among microorganisms.

senses.definition: Organisms that make their own food from simple inorganic substances.
examples: Autotrophs such as plants and algae support many food webs.
In this ecosystem, autotrophs are mostly microscopic algae.

senses.definition: A way of getting food in which an organism makes organic substances from simple inorganic ones.
examples: Autotrophy is essential for life because it provides energy for entire ecosystems.
The shift from autotrophy to heterotrophy can occur during an organism's life cycle.

senses.definition: Growing or turning by itself in response to an internal or external stimulus.
examples: Certain plant roots show autotropic growth even without clear external cues.
Autotropic curvature can occur in stems as they adjust their position.

senses.definition: In a way that involves self-directed growth or movement, often in response to stimuli.
examples: The roots grew autotropically in the absence of light gradients.
Stems may bend autotropically to maintain a stable position.

senses.definition: Growth or turning of a plant organ that is controlled by the organ itself rather than by an obvious external factor.
examples: Autotropism helps roots maintain a straight path in the soil.
Researchers investigated how autotropism interacts with gravity sensing in plants.

senses.definition: A motor truck that moves by its own power.
examples: The factory once used a small autotruck to move goods between buildings.
The museum displays an early autotruck from the 1920s.

senses.definition: A preparation of tuberculin made from a person's own tubercle bacteria, used for testing or treatment.
examples: The doctor considered using autotuberculin for the patient's therapy.
Autotuberculin was once explored as a personalized vaccine approach.

senses.definition: To use software or a device to automatically correct or change the pitch of a singer's voice or an instrument.
examples: They decided to autotune the vocals to fix small pitch problems.
Some artists heavily autotune their voices for a robotic sound.

senses.definition: Software or an effect that automatically corrects or changes musical pitch, especially on voices.
examples: The singer prefers not to use autotune on her records.
Modern pop often relies on autotune as a signature effect.

senses.definition: Uses software or a device to automatically correct or change musical pitch.
examples: The producer autotunes the chorus to achieve a polished sound.
She autotunes her live vocals for consistency.

senses.definition: Automatically turning or rotating without direct human control.
examples: The machine features an autoturning spindle to speed up production.
An autoturning display adjusts its orientation as you rotate the device.

senses.definition: A photographic or printed copy that closely reproduces an original picture or document.
examples: The museum sells an autotype of the famous painting.
Researchers studied an autotype of the original manuscript.

senses.definition: To reproduce something as a close photographic or printed copy.
examples: They autotyped the drawing for inclusion in the catalogue.
Several rare documents were autotyped for the archive.

senses.definition: Reproduced something as a close photographic or printed copy.
examples: The publisher autotyped the original illustrations for the new edition.
Several pages were autotyped to preserve their appearance.
